drafts.hook: Three years on the same vessel.
drafts.body:
Three years on the same vessel.

Every fault recognised by sound. Every supplier who picked up on the first ring. Every workaround that kept a generator running until the part arrived.

None of it in a system.
Nothing logged.

All of it lost.

The next engineer starts from zero.
Not from incompetence. From absence.

The vessel that produced the knowledge cannot retain it. The crew member who learned it cannot leave it behind. The system that should hold it does not know what it does not know.

Built by a former ETO who lived this. The vessel keeps the knowledge.

celeste7.ai

drafts.anchor: /handover verbatim · §10 customer quote (CE March 2026) · §10 (45-min gangway) · skill §6 (chain + handover_doc cards)
drafts.caption:
A chief joins a vessel mid-season. The previous chief is gone. There was no overlap.

The handover doc on the shared drive is a Word file with seven fonts and four authors. The next engineer rebuilds context for three days.

Eight months of operational context, compiled and signed by the outgoing chief, replaces two days of verbal briefing.

celeste7.ai
drafts.alt_text: 9-slide carousel: a chief joins a vessel mid-season with no overlap — the Word-doc handover that breaks under pressure — and the CelesteOS handover that compiles from every operational domain, signed, hyperlinked, ready before the relief opens his laptop. Built by a former ETO. celeste7.ai

slides.h: The handover that wasn't there.
slides.b: A chief joins a vessel mid-season. The previous chief is gone. There was no overlap.

slides.h: The handover doc is a Word file on a shared drive.
slides.b: Multiple authors. Different fonts. Different colours. Updated through the rotation, finalised the night before the outgoing engineer flies home.

slides.h: Written under pressure, by tired people, the night before a flight.
slides.b: Faults marked investigating without signature. Captain requests logged in red without context. Supplier names added without numbers. The record is colour-coded improvisation.

slides.q: The engineers spend three days every rotation rebuilding what the last team knew.
slides.c: Three days × six rotations a year. Weeks of working life, every year, spent reconstructing the rotation they missed.

slides.h: A decade of PMS releases. The handover doc opens the same way it did when it started.
slides.b: The problem has been named for years. The industry has learned to live with it. Until now.

slides.h: The handover, but every line is a link.
Compiled. Signed. Ready when the relief opens his laptop.
Eight months of operational context.
slides.b: Not two days of verbal briefing.

slides.h: Control over your complexity.
slides.b: The handover that wasn't there. Now it is. celeste7.ai

drafts.hook: Ask not what features it has.
drafts.doc_title: Ask not what features it has.
drafts.rationale: Plants the Cross-Domain Chain flag. After posts 1+2 establish founder credibility and product capability, post 3 names the mechanism — the James-Clear-naming-Atomic-Habits move.
drafts.caption:
Every yacht PMS has the modules. Almost none make the connections.

The fault, the work order, the part, the supplier, the audit trail — five rings. Each present in every PMS. Each isolated from the others.

That is cohabitation, not integration.

The Cross-Domain Chain is what a maintenance system looks like when the rings are linked. One fault opens its work order, surfaces its parts, names its supplier, and signs its audit row — without a single module change.

celeste7.ai
drafts.alt_text: 9-slide carousel: every yacht PMS has the same modules — fault, work order, parts, supplier, audit — and almost none of them connect. The Cross-Domain Chain is the five rings linked end to end. Built by a former ETO. celeste7.ai

slides.h: Ask not what features it has.
slides.b: Ask what it connects.

slides.h: Every yacht PMS has a fault module, a work-order module, a parts module, a supplier module.
slides.b: All of them. Side by side. With a shared login.

slides.h: That is not integration.
slides.b: It is the difference between integration and cohabitation.

slides.h: The fault you logged this morning sits in one module.
slides.b: The work order is in another. The part is in a third. The supplier is in a fourth. The audit trail is in a fifth. Nobody links them. Not because nobody wants to. Because nobody built it that way.

slides.h: The intelligence of a maintenance system is not the modules it has.
slides.b: It is the connections it makes.

slides.h: Five rings make the chain.
Break a link, lose the truth.
slides.b: A fault without a work order is an open loop. A work order without parts is a guess. A part without a supplier is an orphan. A supplier without an audit trail is a liability.

slides.h: The Cross-Domain Chain.
slides.b: Not a feature. The way the vessel knows itself.

slides.h: Control over your complexity.
slides.b: celeste7.ai

drafts.hook: Over 60% of chief stewardesses on 60m+ yachts now rotate every 2–3 months.
drafts.body:
Over 60% of chief stewardesses on 60m+ yachts now rotate every 2–3 months.

Two years ago that figure was 40%.

The structural consequence is not turnover. It is operational discontinuity.

Maintenance regimes built for 12-month tenures are now run by people who arrive and leave in 8 weeks. Equipment that was learned over a year is now inherited over a gangway briefing.

This is not a crew problem. It is a system problem.

A vessel that depends on a single chief knowing where every workaround lives, which supplier picks up on the first ring, what the last refit changed but never documented — is a vessel that loses 8 weeks of accumulated knowledge every two months.

The system on the vessel must outlive the crew on the vessel.

That is the new operating reality of yacht engineering.

celeste7.ai

drafts.caption:
A port state inspector steps aboard. A few minutes to produce three years of records.

An invoice is not a record. A signed work order is. A familiarisation log signed at the time, not the morning of the inspection, is.

Every read, every write, every signature: attributed, timestamped, append-only. Independently verifiable in the inspector's own browser at verifier.celeste7.ai.

The audit trail is not a feature. It is a legal defence.

celeste7.ai
drafts.alt_text: 9-slide carousel: a port state inspector steps aboard — the records that exist but do not survive scrutiny — and the CelesteOS audit trail where every action is attributed, timestamped, append-only, and independently verifiable in the inspector's own browser. Built by a former ETO. celeste7.ai

slides.h: When the inspector asks.
slides.b: A port state inspector steps aboard during a routine visit. The captain has a few minutes to produce the records.

slides.h: Every PMS holds records. Few hold defensible ones.
slides.b: Unsigned service entries. Backdated work orders. Familiarisation logs added the morning of the inspection. The records exist. None of them survive scrutiny.

slides.h: An invoice is not a record. A signed work order is.
slides.b: A familiarisation log signed by the crew member is. A handover note that outlives the crew member's departure is. The record is the artifact that can be produced when the question is asked.

slides.h: A denied warranty claim on a main engine runs between €20,000 and €100,000.
slides.b: The maintenance is usually done. The proof is the issue.

slides.h: Records that can be retroactively modified cannot serve as evidence.
slides.b: The audit trail is not a feature. It is a legal defence.

slides.h: Every read. Every write. Every signature.
The inspector asks. The records open.
slides.b: Three years of vessel history in the same view.

slides.h: Independently verifiable.
slides.b: Drop a CelesteOS evidence export at verifier.celeste7.ai. Verification runs entirely in your own browser. No upload. Open-source.

slides.h: Control over your complexity.
slides.b: celeste7.ai
